draft.introduction: The Clouds of Aristophanes (423 BCE) satirizes Socrates and the intellectual culture of Athens, portraying philosophy as a dangerous subversion of traditional values. The play’s caricature of Socrates would later be cited at his trial.

translation.text: Strepsiades: What are these? Tell me. Disciple: This is Astronomy. Strepsiades: And what is this? Disciple: Geometry. Strepsiades: What’s the use of it? Disciple: To measure land. Strepsiades: You mean allotment land? Disciple: No, the whole earth. Strepsiades: A charming notion! What a useful democratic trick!

Socrates: These are the only true deities; all the rest are nonsense. Strepsiades: But what about Zeus on Olympus? Isn’t he a god? Socrates: Zeus? What Zeus? Don’t be absurd. There is no Zeus. Strepsiades: What are you saying? Then who makes it rain? That’s the first thing I want to know.

translator_notes.note: Aristophanes’s Socrates denies the existence of Zeus—a caricature, but one that stuck. Plato’s Apology (18d) has Socrates explicitly address the damage done by this portrayal. The play illustrates how Athenian comedy served as a conservative check on intellectual innovation, weaponizing laughter against philosophy.
